Protein names: Class II O-methyltransferase gedA (EC 2.1.1.283) (Geodin synthesis protein A)
Protein families: Class I-like SAM-binding methyltransferase superfamily, Cation-independent O-methyltransferase family
Domain [CC]: DOMAIN: Active site Asp-374 releases the hydroxyl group on C-1 through competing with the carbonyl group on C-9 and thus destroying intramolecular hydrogen bonds of emodin. Next, His-373, polarized by Glu-446, serves as a general base to deprotonate the hydroxyl group on C-1, thereby activating the nucleophilic hydroxyl group. Subsequently, lone pair of the hydroxyl oxygen on C-1 attacks the positively charged methyl carbon of S-adenosyl-l-methionine (SAM) donor, giving rise to questin with by-product S-adenosyl-L-homocysteine (SAH) released. {ECO:0000269|PubMed:35475366}.
Sequence similarities: SIMILARITY: Belongs to the class I-like SAM-binding methyltransferase superfamily. Cation-independent O-methyltransferase family.
Catalytic activity: CATALYTIC ACTIVITY: Reaction=emodin + S-adenosyl-L-methionine = questin + S-adenosyl-L-homocysteine + H(+); Xref=Rhea:RHEA:36643, ChEBI:CHEBI:15378, ChEBI:CHEBI:57676, ChEBI:CHEBI:57856, ChEBI:CHEBI:59789, ChEBI:CHEBI:77659; EC=2.1.1.283; Evidence={ECO:0000269|PubMed:1444712, ECO:0000269|PubMed:35475366}; PhysiologicalDirection=left-to-right; Xref=Rhea:RHEA:36644; Evidence={ECO:0000269|PubMed:1444712, ECO:0000269|PubMed:35475366}; CATALYTIC ACTIVITY: Reaction=omega-hydroxyemodin + S-adenosyl-L-methionine = questinol + S-adenosyl-L-homocysteine + 2 H(+); Xref=Rhea:RHEA:84819, ChEBI:CHEBI:15378, ChEBI:CHEBI:57856, ChEBI:CHEBI:59789, ChEBI:CHEBI:81348, ChEBI:CHEBI:234006; Evidence={ECO:0000269|PubMed:35475366}; PhysiologicalDirection=left-to-right; Xref=Rhea:RHEA:84820; Evidence={ECO:0000269|PubMed:35475366};
Function [CC]: FUNCTION: Class II O-methyltransferase; part of the gene cluster that mediates the biosynthesis of geodin, an intermediate in the biosynthesis of other natural products (PubMed:1444712, PubMed:24009710, PubMed:35475366). Within the pathway, gedA methylates the C-1 hydroxyl group of emodin to produce questin (PubMed:1444712, PubMed:35475366). GedA is also able to use omega-hydroxyemodin, the hydroxylated derivative of emodin, as a substrate, to convert it into questinol (PubMed:35475366). The pathway begins with the synthesis of atrochrysone thioester by the polyketide synthase (PKS) gedC. The atrochrysone carboxyl ACP thioesterase gedB then breaks the thioester bond and releases the atrochrysone carboxylic acid from gedC. The atrochrysone carboxylic acid is then converted to atrochrysone by gedI which is further transformed into emodinanthrone. The next step is performed by the emodinanthrone oxygenase gedH that catalyzes the oxidation of emodinanthrone to emodin. Emodin O-methyltransferase encoded by gedA then catalyzes methylation of the C-1 hydroxyl group of emodin to form questin. Ring cleavage of questin by questin oxidase gedK leads to desmethylsulochrin via several intermediates including questin epoxide. Another methylation step catalyzed by methyltransferase gedG leads to the formation of sulochrin which is further converted to dihydrogeodin by the sulochrin halogenase gedL. Finally, the dihydrogeodin oxidase gedJ catalyzes the stereospecific phenol oxidative coupling reaction converting dihydrogeodin to geodin (Probable). {ECO:0000269|PubMed:1444712, ECO:0000269|PubMed:24009710, ECO:0000269|PubMed:35475366, ECO:0000305}.

Organism: Aspergillus fumigatus (strain ATCC MYA-4609 / CBS 101355 / FGSC A1100 / Af293) (Neosartorya fumigata)

Catalytic activity: CATALYTIC ACTIVITY: Reaction=emodin + S-adenosyl-L-methionine = questin + S-adenosyl-L-homocysteine + H(+); Xref=Rhea:RHEA:36643, ChEBI:CHEBI:15378, ChEBI:CHEBI:57676, ChEBI:CHEBI:57856, ChEBI:CHEBI:59789, ChEBI:CHEBI:77659; EC=2.1.1.283; Evidence={ECO:0000305|PubMed:26242966}; PhysiologicalDirection=left-to-right; Xref=Rhea:RHEA:36644; Evidence={ECO:0000305|PubMed:26242966};
Function [CC]: FUNCTION: O-methyltransferase; part of the gene cluster that mediates the biosynthesis of trypacidin, a mycotoxin with antiprotozoal activity and that plays a role in the infection process (PubMed:26242966, PubMed:26278536). Trypacidin is toxic for human pulmonary and bronchial epithelial cells by initiating the intracellular formation of nitric oxide (NO) and hydrogen peroxide (H(2)O(2)), thus triggering host necrotic cell death (PubMed:22319557). Within the pathway, tpcA acts as an emodin O-methyltransferase that catalyzes methylation of the 8-hydroxy group of emodin to form questin (PubMed:26242966). The pathway begins with the synthesis of atrochrysone thioester by the polyketide synthase (PKS) tpcC. The atrochrysone carboxyl ACP thioesterase tpcB then breaks the thioester bond and releases the atrochrysone carboxylic acid from tpcC. The decarboxylase tpcK converts atrochrysone carboxylic acid to atrochrysone which is further reduced into emodin anthrone. The next step is performed by the emodin anthrone oxygenase tpcL that catalyzes the oxidation of emodin anthrone to emodin. Emodin O-methyltransferase encoded by tpcA catalyzes methylation of the 8-hydroxy group of emodin to form questin. Ring cleavage of questin by questin oxidase tpcI leads to desmethylsulochrin via several intermediates including questin epoxide. Another methylation step catalyzed by tpcM leads to the formation of sulochrin which is further converted to monomethylsulfochrin by tpcH. Finally, the tpcJ catalyzes the conversion of monomethylsulfochrin to trypacidin. The trypacidin pathway is also able to produce endocrocin via a distinct route from the endocrocin Enc pathway (Probable). {ECO:0000269|PubMed:22319557, ECO:0000269|PubMed:26242966, ECO:0000269|PubMed:26278536, ECO:0000305}.
